title: The nodal intersection problem for Laplace eigenfunctions
speaker: John Toth
abstract: Abstract: I will discuss recent results regarding the problem of counting intersections of eigenfunction nodal sets with real analytic curves H and the connection to eigenfunction restriction bounds over H.

proof_stages.label: Jensen reduction of nodal intersections to restricted mass
proof_stages.summary: The nodal-intersection problem for analytic planar domains and analytic interior curves is reduced, via holomorphic continuation and a Jensen argument, to proving a unique-continuation-type lower bound for the L2 mass of eigenfunctions restricted to the curve.

proof_stages.label: Billiard map as the hypersurface dynamics
proof_stages.summary: For a curve or hypersurface bounding a convex domain, the billiard map on the coball bundle is introduced as the symplectic cross-section dynamics that will later govern restriction and layer-potential operators.

proof_stages.label: Restriction estimates and restricted quantum ergodicity
proof_stages.summary: Known universal and improved upper restriction bounds set the scale, quantum ergodicity is recalled, and restricted quantum ergodicity is stated for hypersurfaces satisfying a microlocal asymmetry/no-perfect-focusing condition under ergodic geodesic flow.

proof_stages.label: Cauchy-data quantum ergodicity linked to ambient QE
proof_stages.summary: To connect directly to a given ambient quantum-ergodic sequence, one uses both Dirichlet data and normalized Neumann data on the hypersurface; the Dirichlet term must be tempered by an operator vanishing at tangential directions, while the Neumann part is uniformly bounded.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to glancing mass and defect measures
proof_stages.summary: Rellich/Cauchy-data identities show that all possible growth of the L2 restriction is microlocalized near the tangential or glancing set, so the problem becomes one of controlling tangential mass, described using semiclassical defect measures.

proof_stages.label: Layer-potential relation and analytic complexification
proof_stages.summary: Green single- and double-layer jump equations relate Dirichlet and Neumann data through operators whose singularities lie on the billiard relation; because direct inversion is too hard, the analytic curve is complexified and weighted holomorphic L2 mass is estimated through boundary matrix elements.

proof_stages.label: Stadium theorem, nodal consequence, and extensions
proof_stages.summary: For the Bunimovich stadium and a strictly convex real-analytic interior curve, every ambient QE sequence has an exponential, indeed polynomial, lower restriction bound, yielding an O(lambda) nodal-intersection bound; the talk closes with open goodness questions and generic asymmetry/flow-out lower-bound results.

examples.name: Highest-weight spherical harmonics on the sphere
examples.note: The highest-weight harmonic on S^2 is used to show that the universal L2 restriction upper bound is sharp and later to illustrate a singular defect measure concentrated on a geodesic.
examples.engagement_reason: computes the highest-weight harmonic’s normalization/eigenvalue scale, exponential concentration off the equator, sharp restriction size, and later its defect measure

passes.reason: highest-weight harmonics are SO(2)-equivariant Gaussian beams at an extreme weight saturating the universal restriction bound
Round-sphere highest-weight modes are highly regular Gaussian beams with maximal concentration along a great circle, saturating the restriction bound rather than refuting it.
Maximal-weight harmonics on the round sphere form a highly regular Gaussian-beam family saturating the universal restriction bound, not refuting it.
highest-weight harmonics are maximally weighted S1-equivariant Gaussian beams on the round sphere and are used only to exhibit sharpness of the restriction bound
Rotationally regular highest-weight Gaussian beams have maximal concentration and saturate the restriction bound, so they are sharpness examples rather than counterexamples.
Highest-weight spherical harmonics are highly regular round-sphere Gaussian beams saturating the sharp restriction bound, not refuting a claim.
Highest-weight harmonics are extremal Gaussian-beam modes saturating the universal restriction bound, not deployed as a counterexample.
highest-weight Gaussian beams are extremal/equivariant and are used to saturate the universal restriction bound

Used to show the universal restriction bound is sharp; highest-weight spherical harmonics are symmetric Gaussian-beam extremizers.
A highly regular highest-weight spherical harmonic is used to show the restriction bound is sharp, not to refute it.

examples.name: Fixed-point hypersurface of an isometry
examples.note: If h is the fixed set of an isometry, odd eigenfunctions vanish on h, giving the obstruction that forces the microlocal asymmetry condition and shows unrestricted goodness is false.
examples.engagement_reason: uses fixed-point hypersurfaces of isometries only as excluded cases where reflection symmetry makes odd eigenfunctions vanish

passes.reason: a mirror fixed hypersurface has maximal reflection symmetry and is used to make odd eigenfunctions vanish on it

A fixed-point hypersurface is a mirror-symmetric locus where odd eigenfunctions vanish, so it is used to show restriction statements fail without an asymmetry condition.
a fixed-point hypersurface is a mirror-symmetric locus where odd eigenfunctions vanish, obstructing the restriction theorem
A reflective fixed hypersurface has maximal symmetry/focusing and is used to show unrestricted restriction goodness fails since odd eigenfunctions vanish on it.
A fixed-point hypersurface has mirror symmetry and odd eigenfunctions vanish on it, obstructing unrestricted QER/lower-bound statements.
A fixed-point hypersurface of an isometry is a mirror-symmetric case used to show the restriction statement fails for odd eigenfunctions.
a reflective fixed hypersurface makes odd eigenfunctions vanish on it, defeating unrestricted restriction or QER claims

Used to show restriction equidistribution/lower bounds can fail because odd eigenfunctions vanish there, with maximal reflection symmetry/focusing.

examples.note: These curves are listed as typical hypersurfaces to which restricted quantum ergodicity applies, except when a geodesic is ruled out by an isometric reflection.

examples.name: Straight line segment on a flat torus
examples.note: The noncurved flat-torus restriction problem is described as reducing to lattice points on circles and sums of two squares, illustrating why dropping strict convexity leads to hard arithmetic issues.
examples.engagement_reason: describes straight line restriction on a flat torus as leading to a hard lattice-point problem, but does not work through that reduction or compute it

passes.reason: a straight torus segment is the zero-curvature affine limiting case rather than a curved hypersurface

examples.name: Boundary of a planar domain as a flow-out case
examples.note: For a planar domain with h equal to the boundary, every relevant trajectory meets h, illustrating the charge-flow-out or nontrapping hypothesis in the generic lower-bound result.
examples.engagement_reason: sets up the planar-domain boundary eigenfunction/nodal-intersection problem and checks the boundary/flow-out situation via unique continuation and the fact that rays hit the boundary
